questions.question: Ferdinand Magellan led the first expedition to circumnavigate the globe (1519-1522). Did Magellan himself complete the voyage?
options: Yes, he completed the full circumnavigation and returned to Spain
No — Magellan was killed in the Philippines in 1521; only 18 of his original 270 crew, led by Juan Sebastián Elcano, completed the voyage
No — he turned back in South America due to storms
Yes, but he died of illness shortly after returning to Spain
questions.answer: No — Magellan was killed in the Philippines in 1521; only 18 of his original 270 crew, led by Juan Sebastián Elcano, completed the voyage
questions.explanation: Ferdinand Magellan's expedition (5 ships, ~270 men) set out in 1519. Magellan was killed in the Battle of Mactan (Philippines) on April 27, 1521 — fighting in a skirmish he didn't need to be in. Juan Sebastián Elcano took command of the one remaining ship (Victoria) and completed the circumnavigation in September 1522 with 18 survivors. The voyage proved Earth's spherical shape and vastness. Three of the five ships were lost; most crew died.
questions.memory_hook: Magellan gets the credit but didn't finish — he was killed in the Philippines by warriors he picked a fight with unnecessarily. Only 18 of 270 men made it back, on 1 of 5 ships. Elcano finished the job.

questions.question: The Meiji Restoration (1868) transformed Japan from a feudal society to a modern industrial nation in record time. What triggered the need for this rapid modernization?
options: A catastrophic earthquake destroyed traditional Japanese infrastructure
Arrival of US Commodore Matthew Perry's 'Black Ships' in 1853, forcing Japan to open to foreign trade under unequal treaties
A revolution by Japanese peasants demanding industrialization
A Chinese invasion that demonstrated Japan's military weakness
questions.answer: Arrival of US Commodore Matthew Perry's 'Black Ships' in 1853, forcing Japan to open to foreign trade under unequal treaties
questions.explanation: Japan had been closed to foreign trade (except limited Dutch contact at Nagasaki) for over 200 years under the Tokugawa shogunate. Perry arrived with 4 iron-hulled steamships in 1853, forcing Japan to sign the Convention of Kanagawa (1854). Confronted with Western technological superiority, the Meiji emperor and reformers abolished the shogunate, launched industrial modernization, and built a modern military — all within decades. Japan went from feudalism to a colonial empire in about 40 years.
questions.memory_hook: Perry's Black Ships arriving in Tokyo Bay in 1853 shocked Japan into modernization — they'd never seen iron steamships. Within 40 years Japan went from samurai to steel mills and became an imperial power.

questions.question: The Scramble for Africa refers to the rapid colonization of the African continent by European powers. During which period did this primarily occur?
questions.explanation: The Scramble for Africa (1884-1914) began at the Berlin Conference (1884-85), where European powers divided Africa without regard for ethnic, cultural, or linguistic boundaries. By 1914, only Ethiopia and Liberia remained independent. Britain, France, Belgium, Germany, Portugal, Italy, and Spain colonized the rest. The borders drawn at Berlin are largely still Africa's national borders today, despite being imposed by outsiders with no local knowledge or consultation. This artificial division is a major source of ongoing conflicts.
questions.memory_hook: At the Berlin Conference (1884), European powers divided Africa like a pie without a single African in the room. The arbitrary borders they drew are essentially unchanged today and remain a source of conflict across the continent.

questions.question: Venus Williams won her first Wimbledon singles title in 2000. How many Wimbledon singles titles did she win total?
questions.explanation: Venus Williams won 5 Wimbledon singles titles: 2000, 2001, 2005, 2007, and 2008. She is one of the greatest players in history, reaching 9 Wimbledon finals total. Venus and her sister Serena Williams together transformed women's tennis with their power and athleticism. Venus also won 7 Grand Slam doubles titles (mostly with Serena) and was a pioneer for equal prize money at Wimbledon and other Grand Slams.
questions.memory_hook: Venus Williams won 5 Wimbledon titles and was instrumental in fighting for equal prize money at Grand Slams. She and Serena changed what women's tennis looked like athletically — bigger, faster, stronger.

questions.question: The Kentucky Derby is horse racing's most famous race in the US, part of the Triple Crown. At which famous track is it held?
options: Belmont Park, New York
Pimlico Race Course, Maryland
Churchill Downs, Louisville, Kentucky
Keeneland, Lexington, Kentucky
questions.explanation: The Kentucky Derby has been run at Churchill Downs in Louisville, Kentucky since 1875, making it the longest continuously running sporting event in the United States. It is the first race in the Triple Crown (followed by the Preakness Stakes at Pimlico and the Belmont Stakes at Belmont Park). The 'Run for the Roses' earns its name from the garland of roses draped over the winning horse. The race is 1.25 miles and typically finishes in about 2 minutes.
questions.memory_hook: The Kentucky Derby at Churchill Downs is America's oldest major sporting event, running since 1875. It's 1.25 miles in about 2 minutes — all the preparation for 120 seconds of racing.

questions.question: The Matrix (1999), directed by the Wachowskis, features a protagonist named Neo who discovers reality is a simulation. What does he choose between — a red pill and a blue pill — and what do they symbolize?
options: Red pill = stay in the Matrix (blissful ignorance); Blue pill = learn the truth about reality
Red pill = learn the truth about reality; Blue pill = remain in the Matrix (blissful ignorance)
Red pill = gain superpowers; Blue pill = return to normal life
Red pill = join the machines; Blue pill = join the resistance
questions.answer: Red pill = learn the truth about reality; Blue pill = remain in the Matrix (blissful ignorance)
questions.explanation: Morpheus offers Neo the choice: the red pill reveals the truth (that the world is a computer simulation controlled by machines), while the blue pill lets him return to his comfortable, ignorant life in the Matrix. The choice has become a cultural shorthand for choosing uncomfortable truth over comfortable illusion. The Matrix was groundbreaking for its 'bullet time' visual effects (freeze-frame 360° rotation). The film stars Keanu Reeves and grossed $463 million on a $63 million budget.
questions.memory_hook: The Matrix's red pill/blue pill has become cultural shorthand for choosing truth over comfortable illusion. The Wachowskis made Neo's choice genuinely hard — most people would probably pick the blue pill.

questions.question: 'Game of Thrones' (HBO, 2011-2019) was based on George R.R. Martin's book series. What is the title of that book series?
options: The Sword of Kings
A Song of Ice and Fire
The Winds of Winter
The Iron Throne Chronicles
questions.answer: A Song of Ice and Fire
questions.explanation: George R.R. Martin's A Song of Ice and Fire book series began with A Game of Thrones (1996). The HBO series ran for 8 seasons and became one of television's most-watched series globally. The show was celebrated for its unpredictable plotting (major characters die without warning) and criticized for its final season's rushed conclusion. Martin has not yet published the 6th or 7th books (The Winds of Winter and A Dream of Spring) despite the series beginning in 1996.
questions.memory_hook: Game of Thrones was actually A Song of Ice and Fire first — the book series started in 1996. Martin is still writing books 6 and 7 (30+ years in). The show outpaced the books and the ending disappointed many fans.

questions.question: Which American author wrote 'Their Eyes Were Watching God' (1937), considered a foundational work of African American literature and the Harlem Renaissance?
options: Alice Walker
Langston Hughes
Zora Neale Hurston
Richard Wright
questions.explanation: Zora Neale Hurston was an anthropologist, folklorist, and author whose novel Their Eyes Were Watching God follows Janie Crawford's journey toward self-determination in rural Florida. The novel was largely overlooked after publication and only rediscovered in the 1970s when Alice Walker championed it. Hurston was a central figure of the Harlem Renaissance and died in poverty in 1960, buried in an unmarked grave until Walker located and marked it in 1973.
questions.memory_hook: Hurston wrote a masterpiece that was ignored for 30 years, died in poverty in an unmarked grave, and was only rediscovered when Alice Walker went looking for her. Their Eyes Were Watching God is now considered essential American literature.

questions.question: The Great Barrier Reef, the world's largest coral reef system, runs along the coast of which Australian state?
options: Western Australia
New South Wales
Victoria
Queensland
questions.explanation: The Great Barrier Reef stretches approximately 2,300 km along the coast of Queensland, northeastern Australia. It is the world's largest coral reef system, comprising over 2,900 individual reefs and 900 islands. It supports extraordinary biodiversity: 1,500 fish species, 4,000 mollusk species, and 240 bird species. Rising ocean temperatures cause coral bleaching — the reef has experienced 5 major bleaching events since 1998. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
questions.memory_hook: The Great Barrier Reef is 2,300 km long — visible from space. Rising ocean temperatures cause bleaching, which kills coral. The reef has had 5 major bleaching events since 1998, threatening its long-term survival.

questions.question: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest landlocked body of water. Which countries border it?
options: Russia, Iran, Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, and Azerbaijan
Russia, Ukraine, Georgia, Turkey, and Armenia
Iran, Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and Bahrain
Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Turkmenistan
questions.answer: Russia, Iran, Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, and Azerbaijan
questions.explanation: The Caspian Sea is bordered by 5 countries: Russia (northwest), Azerbaijan (west), Iran (south), Turkmenistan (east), and Kazakhstan (northeast). Despite being called a 'sea,' it's technically a lake — entirely landlocked with no connection to any ocean. The Caspian holds 40-44% of all lake water on Earth by volume. Its basin holds significant oil and natural gas reserves. The Volga River, Europe's longest river, drains into the Caspian.
questions.memory_hook: The Caspian is a lake that looks like a sea — the world's largest landlocked body of water, with no ocean connection. It sits atop massive oil reserves, making the 5 bordering nations diplomatically important.

questions.question: Olympus Mons on Mars is the largest volcano in the solar system. How does it compare to volcanoes on Earth, and why did it grow so large?
options: Olympus Mons is larger than Mauna Kea in Hawaii but smaller than the Pacific's mid-ocean ridge system — it only appears largest because Mars's surface area is small, making it proportionally dominant

Olympus Mons is 21.9 km tall (nearly three times Everest) and 600 km wide — taller than any mountain on Earth — and grew so large because Mars has no tectonic plates to move the crust away from the hotspot
Olympus Mons is Mars's largest volcano at 15 km tall, similar to Mauna Kea from the ocean floor, but its 300 km base makes it the widest volcanic structure ever measured in the solar system

Olympus Mons grew so large because Mars has an oxygen-rich atmosphere that accelerates volcanic rock deposition — lava oxidizes faster on Mars, adding to the mountain's mass at a rate 10 times faster than Earth volcanoes
questions.answer: Olympus Mons is 21.9 km tall (nearly three times Everest) and 600 km wide — taller than any mountain on Earth — and grew so large because Mars has no tectonic plates to move the crust away from the hotspot
questions.explanation: On Earth, tectonic plates move over volcanic hotspots — which is why the Hawaiian Islands form a chain, with each island progressively older as the plate moved. Mars's lithosphere is stationary (no plate tectonics), so billions of years of eruptions piled onto the same hotspot. Olympus Mons is so wide (600 km) that its edges would be beyond the visible horizon from its summit due to Mars's curvature. Its caldera at the top is 80 km across and 3 km deep. The escarpment (cliff) at its edges is up to 8 km tall — steeper than anything on Earth.
questions.memory_hook: Olympus Mons grew to three times Everest's height because Mars has no tectonic plates — the hotspot never moved, so billions of years of lava piled on the same spot. It's 600 km wide; standing on the summit, you'd be beyond the visible horizon from its edges due to Mars's curvature.

questions.question: What are chemical bonds, and what is the difference between ionic and covalent bonds?
options: Ionic bonds share electrons equally; covalent bonds transfer electrons between atoms
Ionic bonds form when electrons are transferred between atoms (creating charged ions); covalent bonds form when atoms share electrons
Ionic bonds occur only in gases; covalent bonds occur only in liquids
Both bond types involve metal atoms; the difference is whether the metal is positively or negatively charged
questions.answer: Ionic bonds form when electrons are transferred between atoms (creating charged ions); covalent bonds form when atoms share electrons
questions.explanation: Ionic bonds: one atom loses an electron (becomes positively charged) and another gains it (becomes negatively charged). The opposite charges attract. Example: sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl) form table salt (NaCl). Covalent bonds: atoms share electrons. Example: two hydrogen atoms share electrons to form H₂; oxygen shares with two hydrogens to form H₂O. Covalent bonds are generally stronger and found in biological molecules (DNA, proteins, sugars).
questions.memory_hook: Ionic bonds: electron theft (Na gives to Cl → salt). Covalent bonds: electron sharing (atoms holding electrons together). The difference explains why salt dissolves in water but organic molecules like fats don't — covalent bonds hold them together differently.
